# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains comparative benchmark-description evidence: benchmark scope, dataset composition, evaluation method, language coverage, contamination caveats, and stated limits on what benchmark scores do and do not measure.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-016
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: claim-support
citation: Raykar, nilenso blog, 2025-09-25
source_url: https://blog.nilenso.com/blog/2025/09/25/swe-benchmarks/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
SWE-bench measures whether a coding agent can submit a patch for a real GitHub issue that passes the issue’s unit tests.

## Source Excerpt

The source says SWE-bench measures "How well a coding agent can submit a patch for a real-world GitHub issue that passes the unit tests for that issue."

## Note

This evidence states the benchmark’s stated target, not whether that target matches broader software engineering work.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-016
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: metric
citation: Raykar, nilenso blog, 2025-09-25
source_url: https://blog.nilenso.com/blog/2025/09/25/swe-benchmarks/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
SWE-bench Verified has 500 problems and all are in Python.

## Source Excerpt

The source states: "It has 500 problems, all in Python."

## Note

This is a dataset composition fact about SWE-bench Verified.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-016
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: metric
citation: Raykar, nilenso blog, 2025-09-25
source_url: https://blog.nilenso.com/blog/2025/09/25/swe-benchmarks/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
More than 40% of SWE-bench Verified issues come from the Django repository, and web applications are entirely missing.

## Source Excerpt

The source says: "Over 40% are issues from the Django source repository; the rest are libraries. Web applications are entirely missing."

## Note

This introduces a coverage limitation in repository mix and application type.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-016
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: metric
citation: Raykar, nilenso blog, 2025-09-25
source_url: https://blog.nilenso.com/blog/2025/09/25/swe-benchmarks/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
SWE-bench Verified solutions are small, with mean solution size 11 lines of code and median solution size 4 lines.

## Source Excerpt

The source states: "The mean lines of code per solution are 11, and median lines of code are 4."

## Note

This is a size statistic for benchmark solutions, not a quality judgment.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-016
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: Raykar, nilenso blog, 2025-09-25
source_url: https://blog.nilenso.com/blog/2025/09/25/swe-benchmarks/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Amazon found that more than 77.6% of SWE-bench Verified solutions touch only one function.

## Source Excerpt

The source says: "Amazon found that over 77.6% of the solutions touch only one function."

## Note

This describes the locality of benchmark fixes.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-016
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: incident
citation: Raykar, nilenso blog, 2025-09-25
source_url: https://blog.nilenso.com/blog/2025/09/25/swe-benchmarks/
confidence: medium
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Because SWE-bench Verified issues are from 2023 and earlier, the author says the data was almost certainly in training sets and improvements are hard to separate from memorization.

## Source Excerpt

The source states: "All the issues are from 2023 and earlier. This data was almost certainly in the training sets. Thus it’s hard to tell how much of the improvements are due to memorisation."

## Note

This is a contamination caveat that limits attribution of benchmark gains to general problem-solving rather than training exposure.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-016
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: metric
citation: Raykar, nilenso blog, 2025-09-25
source_url: https://blog.nilenso.com/blog/2025/09/25/swe-benchmarks/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
SWE-bench Pro contains 1,865 problems from 41 repositories.

## Source Excerpt

The source says: "1865 problems, from 41 repositories."

## Note

This is a scale and source-count fact for SWE-bench Pro.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-016
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: claim-support
citation: Raykar, nilenso blog, 2025-09-25
source_url: https://blog.nilenso.com/blog/2025/09/25/swe-benchmarks/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
SWE-bench Pro uses Python, Go, JavaScript, and TypeScript repositories, but the source says their distribution is not uniform and does not disclose the exact mix.

## Source Excerpt

The source states: "The repositories are Python, Go, JS and TS—we don’t know the distribution, except that they are 'not uniform'."

## Note

This preserves an explicit uncertainty about language distribution.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-016
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: metric
citation: Raykar, nilenso blog, 2025-09-25
source_url: https://blog.nilenso.com/blog/2025/09/25/swe-benchmarks/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
SWE-bench Pro solutions have mean size 107 lines of code, median size 55 lines, and span an average of 4 files.

## Source Excerpt

The source says: "The solutions have a mean of 107 lines of code, and a median of 55 lines of code, and usually span an average of 4 files."

## Note

This is a complexity and spread statistic for SWE-bench Pro tasks.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-016
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: method
citation: Raykar, nilenso blog, 2025-09-25
source_url: https://blog.nilenso.com/blog/2025/09/25/swe-benchmarks/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
SWE-bench Pro uses human-rewritten problems grounded in issues, commits, and pull requests to reduce missing information.

## Source Excerpt

The source states: "They actually got humans to rewrite problems based on issues, commits and PRs to ensure there’s no missing information."

## Note

This describes benchmark construction method, not outcome quality.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-016
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: method
citation: Raykar, nilenso blog, 2025-09-25
source_url: https://blog.nilenso.com/blog/2025/09/25/swe-benchmarks/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
SWE-bench Pro provides dockerized environments with dependencies already installed, so it does not test repository setup ability.

## Source Excerpt

The source says: "They also have dockerized environments set up with all the dependencies installed and configured, so the benchmark explicitly does not test if your agent can setup your repository."

## Note

This is a scope boundary on what the benchmark excludes.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-016
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: quote
citation: Raykar, nilenso blog, 2025-09-25
source_url: https://blog.nilenso.com/blog/2025/09/25/swe-benchmarks/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The source defines a 25% SWE-bench Pro score as 25% of well-defined benchmark issues producing solutions whose unit tests pass.

## Source Excerpt

The source says: "So when we say that an agent scores 25% in SWE-bench Pro, we are saying: 'In a problem set of well-defined issues with pointed requirements and (the occasional) specification of code interfaces, 25% of the solutions from the agent get the respective problem’s unit test cases to pass'."

## Note

This keeps the benchmark interpretation tied to well-defined issues and unit-test validation only.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-016
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: claim-support
citation: Raykar, nilenso blog, 2025-09-25
source_url: https://blog.nilenso.com/blog/2025/09/25/swe-benchmarks/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The source says SWE-bench results do not show whether code is maintainable, secure, provably correct, or well-crafted; they show only that unit tests pass.

## Source Excerpt

The source states: "With this benchmark we do not have any idea if the code is maintainable, secure, provably correct, or well-crafted—we just know that the unit test cases for it will pass."

## Note

This is an explicit limit on what benchmark success establishes about software quality.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-016
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: claim-support
citation: Raykar, nilenso blog, 2025-09-25
source_url: https://blog.nilenso.com/blog/2025/09/25/swe-benchmarks/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Aider Polyglot measures whether Aider can solve hard Exercism problems and make file edits that pass unit tests after at most one round of feedback.

## Source Excerpt

The source says Aider Polyglot measures: "If the coding agent (specifically, Aider) can solve hard-level Exercism problems and apply file edits that pass unit tests after at most one round of feedback."

## Note

This evidence describes the benchmark target and evaluation setting.
```

### file: evidence-15.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-016
evidence_id: evidence-15
kind: metric
citation: Raykar, nilenso blog, 2025-09-25
source_url: https://blog.nilenso.com/blog/2025/09/25/swe-benchmarks/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Aider Polyglot covers 225 problems across six languages: C++, Go, Java, JavaScript, Python, and Rust.

## Source Excerpt

The source lists: "C++ 26, Go 39, Java 47, JavaScript 49, Python 34, Rust 30, Total 225."

## Note

This is a language-coverage and problem-count fact.
```

### file: evidence-16.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-21-016
evidence_id: evidence-16
kind: claim-support
citation: Raykar, nilenso blog, 2025-09-25
source_url: https://blog.nilenso.com/blog/2025/09/25/swe-benchmarks/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
LiveCodeBench measures Python competitive-programming performance under hidden test suites using a rolling fresh problem set.

## Source Excerpt

The source says: "Python competitive-programming skills under hidden test suites with a rolling, 'fresh' problem set. Think LeetCode." It also states: "Everything is Python."

## Note

This evidence is limited to the benchmark’s stated domain and setup.

Because SWE-bench Verified issues are from 2023 and earlier, the author says the data was almost certainly in training sets and improvements are hard to separate from memorization.

## Source Excerpt

The source states: "All the issues are from 2023 and earlier. This data was almost certainly in the training sets. Thus it’s hard to tell how much of the improvements are due to memorisation."

## Note

This is a contamination caveat that limits attribution of benchmark gains to general problem-solving rather than training exposure.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---

article_id: art-2026-03-21-016
evidence_id: evidence-12

citation: Raykar, nilenso blog, 2025-09-25
source_url: https://blog.nilenso.com/blog/2025/09/25/swe-benchmarks/
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
The source defines a 25% SWE-bench Pro score as 25% of well-defined benchmark issues producing solutions whose unit tests pass.

## Source Excerpt

The source says: "So when we say that an agent scores 25% in SWE-bench Pro, we are saying: 'In a problem set of well-defined issues with pointed requirements and (the occasional) specification of code interfaces, 25% of the solutions from the agent get the respective problem’s unit test cases to pass'."

## Note

This keeps the benchmark interpretation tied to well-defined issues and unit-test validation only.
```
